title: Solar dyeing in a jar
subtitle: Using sunlight and a sealed jar to dye small amounts of fibre in plant dyes
excerpt: Solar jar dyeing uses sunlight rather than a heat source to fix natural plant dyes. Small amounts of alum-mordanted fibre are sealed in a jar with the dye bath and left in full sun for several days. The method is low-energy, requires minimal equipment, and is well suited to sampling small amounts of multiple colours.
sourceNotes: Solar jar dyeing synthesised from contemporary natural dye practice. The method is documented in Rebecca Burgess, Fibershed (2019, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and widely described in British natural dye guild teaching as a low-energy sampling technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt that forms a bond between the wool fibre and the dye. The fibre must be mordanted before entering the solar jar for the dye to fix permanently.
The liquid containing the extracted plant dye. For solar jar dyeing, the dye bath is made at room temperature or warmed slightly before going into the jar.

content.text: Solar jar dyeing replaces the dye-pot heat source with summer sunlight. An alum-
fibre sample is sealed inside a jar with the prepared

and placed in full sun. The glass acts as a small greenhouse, building internal temperatures to 50 to 70 degrees Celsius on a sunny summer day without any additional heat. The slow, gentle heat over 3 to 5 days gives a well-penetrated, relatively even colour.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Use dedicated jars. Wear gloves when handling mordant baths. Alum is low-hazard; keep out of reach of food and children.

items.name: Plant dye material (fresh or dried, see notes)
Lidded glass jar (1 to 2 litre kilner jar or similar)

content.text: Dissolve alum in warm water and mordant the fibre in a standard dye-pot at 80 degrees Celsius for 45 minutes. This step requires a heat source; it is the preparation step, not the solar step. Allow the mordanted fibre to cool and squeeze gently before putting it into the jar.
Step 2: Prepare the dye bath in the jar

Place the plant dye material directly in the jar: fresh or dried flowers, chopped leaves, or a measured amount of ground spice. Add enough water at room temperature to cover everything loosely. Good choices for solar jar dyeing include onion skins, chamomile flowers, goldenrod, and weld leaves. Place the mordanted fibre on top.
Step 3: Seal and place in sun

Seal the jar tightly and place it in a position that receives full sun for as many hours per day as possible. Leave for 3 to 7 days. Turn the jar daily to redistribute the fibre and even out the colour. Onion skin jars will show colour after 1 to 2 days; chamomile and lighter plant dyes may need 5 to 7 days.

Remove the fibre from the jar and rinse in cool water until the water runs clear. The colour will be slightly lighter once dry. Record the plant material, mordant, and number of days for future reference.
The spent dye bath and plant material from an alum-and-plant solar jar can be composted. Small quantities of alum mordant bath can go via the foul drain.

items.problem: The colour is very uneven, with one side of the fibre much darker than the other
items.cause: The jar was not turned regularly enough during solar dyeing
items.fix: Turn the jar twice daily and ensure the fibre is fully submerged. The fibre facing the jar lid gets the least heat exposure.

items.problem: No colour has developed after 5 days
items.cause: Cloudy weather reduced the solar temperature below effective dyeing range
items.fix: On cloudy days, bring the jar indoors to a warm south-facing windowsill. The temperature needs to reach at least 40 degrees Celsius for the dye to fix.
